questions.question_text: A seller receives a written offer from a buyer. The seller makes changes to the price and initials them, then signs and returns the document to the buyer. At this point:
questions.correct_answer: A counteroffer was made, terminating the original offer
distractors: A binding contract was formed when both parties signed
The original offer remains valid and open
The counteroffer is void for improper format
questions.explanation: Offer and acceptance (mutual assent) require a 'mirror image' — the acceptance must match the offer exactly. When a seller modifies an offer's terms, they have rejected the original offer and created a counteroffer. This terminates the original offer — the buyer cannot later go back and accept the original terms. The counteroffer puts the buyer in the decision-making position: accept the new terms, reject them, or submit another counter. A contract is only formed when one party unequivocally accepts the other's exact terms.

questions.question_text: A 16-year-old signs a contract to purchase a home. This contract is:
questions.explanation: Contracts with minors are voidable, not void. A minor (under 18 in most states) may disaffirm (cancel) the contract at any time before reaching majority, or for a reasonable time after. However, the contract is not automatically void — the minor may also choose to honor it. The adult party cannot use the minor's incapacity to void the contract; only the minor has that right. Other parties lacking contractual capacity include: persons adjudicated mentally incompetent (contracts are void, not voidable) and persons under the influence at the time of signing (voidable).

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller sign a purchase contract. The buyer's consideration is:
questions.correct_answer: The promise to pay the agreed purchase price
distractors: Love and affection between family members transferring property
The buyer's pre-approval letter from their lender
questions.explanation: Consideration is something of value exchanged by each party. In a purchase contract, the buyer's consideration is the promise to pay (or the actual payment of) the purchase price. The seller's consideration is the promise to convey (transfer) the property. Consideration does not have to be money — it can be any legal benefit to the promisor or detriment to the promisee. However, 'love and affection' is generally not sufficient legal consideration for a real estate contract, though it may support a gift deed. The earnest money deposit is partial consideration but not the whole of it.

questions.question_text: A broker's listing agreement specifies that the broker will be paid a commission only if the property sells to a buyer who is an undocumented immigrant. This contract is:
questions.explanation: Lawful purpose (legality of object) is a required element of a valid contract. A contract that requires or is conditioned on illegal discrimination violates the Fair Housing Act and is void — it has no legal effect from the moment it is created and cannot be ratified or enforced by either party. This differs from a voidable contract (which is valid until one party elects to cancel) or an unenforceable contract (which is valid between the parties but a court will not help enforce it). Any contract requiring illegal acts is void.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to sell their home because the buyer threatened to expose a personal secret unless the seller accepted. The seller later sues to cancel the contract. The seller will likely succeed because the contract lacked:
questions.explanation: Voluntary consent requires that all parties enter a contract freely, without duress, menace, fraud, undue influence, or mistake. Duress (physical threats or unlawful pressure) and menace (threats of harm) both vitiate consent, making the contract voidable at the option of the coerced party. Undue influence involves improper persuasion by a person in a position of trust or authority. Fraud involves intentional misrepresentation of a material fact. In all these cases, the injured party may elect to affirm or disaffirm (rescind) the contract.

questions.question_text: Under the Statute of Frauds, which of the following real estate agreements is ENFORCEABLE even without being in writing?
questions.correct_answer: A short-term lease for one year or less in most states
distractors: A buyer representation agreement lasting six months
A purchase contract for a vacant lot
A listing agreement to sell a single-family home
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable — including contracts for the sale or transfer of real property, real estate listing agreements, leases for more than one year, and buyer representation agreements. Most states exempt short-term leases (one year or less) from the writing requirement as a practical exception for common residential arrangements. The three incorrect options are all agreements that must be in writing: purchase contracts involve the transfer of real property; buyer rep agreements and listing agreements are real estate brokerage contracts.

questions.question_text: The 'mirror image rule' in contract law means that:
questions.correct_answer: An acceptance must match the offer exactly
distractors: Both parties must sign identical copies of the contract simultaneously
The listing price must equal the appraised value for validity
Buyer and seller must each receive identical copies of closing documents
questions.explanation: The mirror image rule requires that an acceptance match the offer in every detail. If the offeree changes any term — price, closing date, contingencies, personal property included — the response is a counteroffer, not an acceptance. The original offer is terminated and cannot be accepted later. This rule ensures that both parties have genuinely agreed to the same terms (mutual assent). In practice, real estate negotiations often involve multiple counteroffers before terms are matched.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer that states 'this offer expires at 5:00 PM Friday.' On Saturday morning, the seller signs and delivers the acceptance. The result is:
questions.explanation: An offer can only be accepted while it is still open. When an offer specifies an expiration time, it automatically terminates at that time if not accepted. The seller's late signing is not an acceptance — it is a new offer from the seller to the buyer, which the buyer can accept or reject. If no expiration time is stated, the offer remains open for a 'reasonable time,' which varies by circumstance. The offeror can also revoke an offer at any time before acceptance (unless consideration was paid to keep it open, as in an option).

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer on Monday. Before the seller responds, the buyer calls and says 'I withdraw my offer.' The buyer's action is called:
questions.explanation: Revocation is the withdrawal of an offer by the offeror before it has been accepted. An offeror can revoke at any time before acceptance, even if the offer states it will remain open for a certain period (unless the offer is supported by consideration, making it an option contract). Revocation must be communicated to the offeree to be effective. Rescission is the cancellation of an existing contract. Rejection is the offeree's refusal. No breach occurs because no contract existed yet.

questions.question_text: Which of the following will NOT terminate an offer?
questions.correct_answer: The offeree asking questions about terms without proposing changes
distractors: The offeree making a counteroffer with different terms
The offeror revoking the offer before acceptance is communicated
The death of the offeror before the offeree has accepted
questions.explanation: An offer is terminated by: rejection, counteroffer, revocation, expiration of time, death or incapacity of either party, or destruction of the subject property. However, merely asking questions or requesting information about the offer does not constitute a rejection or counteroffer. The offeree might ask 'Would you consider including the appliances?' without that being a counteroffer — it is an inquiry. Only a definitive change in terms or a conditional acceptance creates a counteroffer.

questions.question_text: A person who has been legally adjudicated (declared by a court) as mentally incompetent signs a contract. This contract is:
questions.explanation: There is an important distinction: a person adjudicated incompetent (court order) enters void contracts — they have no legal effect. A person who is mentally impaired but has not been court-declared incompetent enters voidable contracts — they or their guardian may choose to cancel. Minors also enter voidable contracts. The difference matters: void contracts cannot be ratified; voidable contracts can be affirmed or disaffirmed by the protected party.

questions.question_text: A person signs a purchase contract while heavily intoxicated. The next day, while sober, they confirm they want to proceed. The contract is now:
questions.explanation: A contract signed while intoxicated is voidable, not void — the impaired party can disaffirm it or ratify it. Ratification occurs when the party, after regaining capacity, confirms their intent to be bound. Once ratified, the contract becomes fully enforceable. The key distinction: void contracts (court-declared incompetent) cannot be ratified. Voidable contracts (minors, intoxicated persons, undue influence) can be either affirmed or disaffirmed by the protected party.

questions.question_text: Which of the following persons has full legal capacity to enter into a real estate contract?
questions.correct_answer: An 18-year-old who has graduated high school and works full-time
distractors: A 17-year-old who has been legally emancipated by the court
A 15-year-old with parental consent to purchase property
A person under court-appointed guardianship for cognitive disability
questions.explanation: An 18-year-old has reached the age of majority in most states and has full contractual capacity. A 17-year-old emancipated minor may have capacity in some states but this varies by jurisdiction — emancipation does not universally grant full contractual capacity. Parental consent does not cure a minor's lack of capacity. A person under guardianship has been determined by the court to lack capacity, so their guardian must act on their behalf. The safest answer on the exam is the adult over 18.

questions.question_text: A contract signed by a minor is voidable. This means:
questions.correct_answer: The minor may cancel the contract, but the adult party cannot
distractors: Either party may cancel because one party lacks legal capacity
The contract is automatically void with no legal effect
The contract is valid but a court will refuse to enforce it
questions.explanation: Voidable means the contract is valid and enforceable unless the protected party (the minor) chooses to disaffirm it. Only the minor has this right — the adult party is fully bound. This protects the minor from exploitation while allowing them to benefit from contracts they want to keep. A void contract, by contrast, has no legal effect from the start and cannot be enforced by either party. The adult's inability to cancel is designed to prevent adults from taking advantage of the minor's age.

questions.question_text: Valuable consideration in a real estate contract must be:
distractors: Equal in dollar value to the property being sold
At least 10% of the purchase price to show serious intent
questions.explanation: Consideration must have legally recognized value but need not be adequate or equal to the other party's consideration. Courts generally do not evaluate the fairness of a bargain — $1 can be valid consideration for a property transfer (though extreme disparity might suggest fraud or duress). Consideration can take many forms: money, a promise to act or refrain from acting, property, services, or the release of a legal claim. Good consideration (love and affection) may support a gift but is generally insufficient for an enforceable contract.

questions.question_text: A father transfers his home to his daughter 'for love and affection' with no monetary payment. This transfer:
questions.correct_answer: Is a valid gift but lacks valuable consideration for a binding contract
distractors: Is void because real property can only transfer through a sale at market value
Creates a binding contract because love and affection are valid consideration
Requires court approval because transfers without payment are presumed fraudulent
questions.explanation: Love and affection is 'good consideration' (natural consideration arising from a relationship) but not 'valuable consideration' (something with measurable economic value). A deed given for love and affection is a valid gift — the transfer is effective. However, it does not create a binding contract because there is no valuable consideration exchanged. This distinction matters if the transferor later wants to take the property back — without a binding contract, the gift may be harder to challenge but also harder to enforce as a contractual obligation.

questions.question_text: In a real estate purchase contract, the seller's consideration is:
questions.correct_answer: The promise to convey title to the property at closing
distractors: The earnest money deposit received from the buyer's agent
The commission paid to the real estate broker at settlement
The property tax proration credit given to the buyer at closing
questions.explanation: In a bilateral purchase contract, each party provides consideration. The buyer's consideration is the promise to pay the purchase price. The seller's consideration is the promise to convey (transfer) title to the property. The earnest money is part of the buyer's consideration, not the seller's. The broker's commission is a separate obligation under the listing agreement, not the purchase contract's consideration. Tax prorations are adjustments at closing, not the basis of the contractual exchange.

questions.question_text: An existing debt can serve as valid consideration for a contract. This means:
questions.correct_answer: A promise to forgive a debt can serve as consideration for property
distractors: Only cash or cash equivalents qualify as consideration in real estate
Debts must be fully paid before either party can enter a new contract
Past consideration from a prior transaction is always sufficient
questions.explanation: Consideration can take many forms, including the forgiveness or discharge of an existing debt. If A owes B $50,000, B could agree to forgive the debt in exchange for A conveying a property — the debt forgiveness is B's consideration, and the property is A's. However, 'past consideration' (something already given before the new promise) is generally not valid consideration for a new contract. Consideration must be bargained for in the current exchange.

questions.question_text: A contract for the sale of a property that will be used as a drug manufacturing facility is:
questions.explanation: One of the essential elements of a valid contract is lawful purpose (legality of object). If the contract itself requires or is designed to facilitate an illegal act, it is void from inception — neither party can enforce it. However, a standard purchase contract is not void merely because the buyer secretly plans illegal use — the contract's stated purpose (buying property) is legal. The contract would be void if the illegal purpose is integral to the agreement's terms, such as 'purchase price includes equipment for illegal manufacturing.'

questions.question_text: The four essential elements of a valid contract are:
questions.correct_answer: Competent parties, mutual assent, consideration, and lawful purpose
distractors: Written form, notarized signatures, earnest money, and legal description
Offer price, inspection contingency, financing contingency, and closing date
questions.explanation: Every valid contract requires four elements: (1) competent parties — legal capacity to contract; (2) mutual assent — agreement through offer and acceptance; (3) consideration — something of value exchanged; (4) lawful purpose — the contract must not violate law or public policy. While written form is required for real estate contracts under the Statute of Frauds, that is a requirement for enforceability, not validity. A contract missing any of the four essential elements is either void or voidable.

questions.question_text: A seller and buyer enter into a contract to sell a property at an artificially low price to avoid transfer taxes. This contract has:
questions.explanation: While parties generally can agree to any price, a contract specifically structured to evade taxes has an unlawful purpose. Tax evasion (intentional fraud) is illegal, and a contract designed to facilitate it may be void or unenforceable. Tax avoidance (using legal strategies to minimize taxes) is different from evasion (illegally hiding or misrepresenting facts). Courts look at the intent: was the low price a genuine bargain, or was it a scheme to defraud taxing authorities? Both parties face potential criminal and civil penalties.

questions.question_text: A contract for the sale of a property that does not exist (it was destroyed by fire before the contract was signed) is:
questions.explanation: If the subject matter of a contract is destroyed before the contract is formed, the contract is void due to impossibility — there is nothing to sell. This is different from destruction after contract formation (where the risk-of-loss rules determine which party bears the loss). The land still exists, but if the contract was specifically for the improved property (building included), the destruction of the building eliminates the subject matter. Mutual mistake of fact about a fundamental condition also makes the contract voidable.

questions.question_text: A real estate agent tells a buyer that the home has a new roof, knowing the roof is 20 years old and leaking. The buyer relies on this statement and purchases the home. The buyer may seek to cancel the contract based on:
questions.explanation: Fraud requires four elements: (1) a false statement of material fact, (2) knowledge of its falsity (scienter), (3) intent to induce reliance, and (4) actual reliance by the injured party resulting in damages. Here, the agent knowingly lied about a material fact (roof condition) to get the buyer to purchase. The buyer relied on this misrepresentation. This is not duress (no force/threats), mistake (the agent knew the truth), or undue influence (no exploitation of a fiduciary or trust relationship).

questions.question_text: An elderly homeowner's caretaker persuades them to sell their home at a below-market price to the caretaker's relative. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Undue influence occurs when someone in a position of trust or authority improperly persuades another person to act against their own interest. Caretakers, attorneys, family members, and others in positions of trust can exert undue influence over vulnerable persons. Unlike duress (which involves threats or force), undue influence involves subtle persuasion and exploitation of a relationship. The resulting contract is voidable at the option of the influenced party or their legal representative.

questions.question_text: Both the buyer and seller honestly believe a property contains 10 acres when it actually contains only 7 acres. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: A mutual mistake occurs when both parties share the same material misunderstanding. When the mistake involves a fundamental fact (like property size), the contract is typically voidable by either party. A unilateral mistake (only one party is wrong) generally does not allow rescission unless the other party knew of the mistake. Fraud requires intentional deception — here, both parties honestly believed the wrong figure. Puffery involves subjective opinions ('great location'), not objective facts like acreage.

questions.question_text: A contract obtained through duress is:
questions.correct_answer: Voidable by the party who was coerced
questions.explanation: Duress makes a contract voidable, not void. The coerced party has the option to disaffirm (cancel) the contract or to affirm it if they decide the terms are actually acceptable. Only the coerced party has this right — the party who applied the duress remains fully bound. This differs from contracts that are void (such as those with an illegal purpose or signed by a court-declared incompetent person), which have no legal effect regardless of either party's wishes.

questions.question_text: The Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing. Its primary purpose is to:
questions.correct_answer: Prevent fraudulent claims by requiring written evidence of important agreements
distractors: Ensure all parties hire an attorney before signing real estate contracts
Prohibit oral agreements between real estate licensees and clients
Require contracts be filed with the county recorder to be enforceable
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds (dating back to English common law in 1677) requires written evidence of certain important agreements to prevent people from falsely claiming that oral agreements exist. For real estate, this includes contracts for the sale of land, leases over one year, and listing/buyer representation agreements. The statute does not require attorneys, does not prohibit all oral agreements, and does not require recording. An oral real estate contract may be valid between the parties but unenforceable in court.

questions.question_text: Under the Statute of Frauds, which of the following must be in writing to be enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: A two-year residential lease agreement between a landlord and tenant
distractors: A month-to-month rental agreement with no fixed term specified
An oral agreement between neighbors about a shared driveway use
A verbal promise to mow a neighbor's lawn in exchange for $50
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires writing for leases exceeding one year. A two-year lease must be in writing to be enforceable. A month-to-month agreement is typically for less than one year and does not require writing. An oral agreement about driveway use and a verbal lawn-mowing promise are not the types of contracts the Statute of Frauds addresses — they do not involve the sale or long-term leasing of real property. The Statute applies to: land sales, leases over one year, and other specified categories.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller have an oral agreement for the sale of a house. The buyer moves in, pays the full price, and the seller delivers the deed. Can the Statute of Frauds void this sale?
questions.explanation: The doctrine of part performance (or full performance) is an exception to the Statute of Frauds. When parties have fully performed an oral real estate contract — payment made, deed delivered, possession transferred — courts generally will not allow either party to undo the transaction by invoking the Statute of Frauds. The purpose of the statute is to prevent fraud, and allowing a party to take back a fully performed deal would itself be fraudulent. Part performance (partial payment, possession, improvements) can also satisfy the statute in many jurisdictions.

questions.question_text: To satisfy the Statute of Frauds, a written real estate contract must include:
distractors: A notarized acknowledgment by both parties before a notary public
Signatures of two disinterested witnesses who observed the signing
A legal opinion letter from an attorney confirming enforceability
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires a written memorandum containing the essential terms of the agreement: identification of the parties, description of the property, the price or consideration, and the signature of the party to be charged (the party against whom enforcement is sought). Notarization is required for recording a deed but not for contract enforceability. Witnesses are required for deeds in some states but not typically for purchase contracts. Attorney review is optional in most states.
